g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Cache Coherence in Parallel Programming. We'll explore how multiprocessors maintain consistent data views.
Cache coherence ensures all processors see consistent data. When caches hold copies of the same memory location, updates must be visible to all.
g.text: Multiple processors maintain local caches for faster memory access. Cache coherence ensures all
processors see a consistent view of shared data. Without it, updates by one processor may
be invisible to others, causing incorrect program behavior in parallel systems.

g.data-narration: Here's the core problem: Processor 1 updates X to 10, but Processor 2 still reads 5 from its stale cache copy. This inconsistency breaks correctness.
Write-through writes to memory immediately, ensuring consistency. Write-back delays writes, reducing traffic but requiring careful coherence management.
Snooping protocols have all cache controllers monitor the shared bus. When a write occurs, other caches see it and take action to maintain coherence.
g.text: All cache controllers monitor (snoop) the shared bus for memory transactions.
When a processor writes to shared data, other caches observe and react.

g.data-narration: Write-invalidate removes other copies on write (lower traffic, higher latency). Write-update modifies all copies immediately (higher traffic, lower latency).
g.text: Write - Broadcast INVALIDATE to other caches
Next read by others - Cache miss, fetch new value
Write - Broadcast UPDATED value to all caches
All caches immediately have correct value

g.data-narration: Directory-based protocols scale better. A central directory tracks which caches hold each memory block, sending targeted invalidations instead of broadcasting.
g.text: For larger systems, snooping becomes inefficient. A central directory tracks cache ownership.
Directory sends targeted messages only to relevant caches, not broadcast.

g.data-narration: MESI has four states: Modified (dirty, exclusive), Exclusive (clean, exclusive), Shared (clean, may exist elsewhere), and Invalid (stale data).
g.text: MESI (Modified-Exclusive-Shared-Invalid) is a widely-used write-invalidate protocol.
Dirty = Modified (different from memory) | Clean = Same as memory

g.data-narration: MESI state transitions occur on reads and writes. Modified writes locally; Shared invalidates others; Invalid fetches from memory or obtains ownership.
False sharing occurs when unrelated variables share a cache line. Writing to one invalidates the entire line, causing performance degradation.
g.text: False Sharing: The Hidden Performance Killer
Different variables on the same cache line cause unnecessary invalidations.
CPU1 writes x - Invalidates line in CPU2
Even though CPU2 only accesses y
Solution: Padding to align variables to separate cache lines

g.data-narration: Cache coherence adds overhead: bus traffic, synchronization latency, and hardware complexity. Impact depends on sharing patterns and protocol choice.
g.text: Impact depends on: Sharing pattern | Protocol choice | Number of processors

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: Cache coherence prevents stale data in multiprocessors. MESI is the standard protocol. Beware of false sharing for performance. Choose protocols based on system size.
g.text: 1. Cache coherence ensures consistent data views across processor caches
2. MESI: Modified (dirty) - Exclusive (clean, exclusive) - Shared (clean, shared) - Invalid (stale)
3. Snooping for small systems, Directory-based for scalability | Beware false sharing

g.data-narration: For exams: Trace MESI transitions, compare protocols, identify false sharing, and remember coherence does not solve race conditions.
g.text: Be able to trace MESI state transitions for given read/write patterns
Compare snooping vs directory-based: broadcast vs point-to-point, scalability
Remember: coherence is not synchronization (does not prevent race conditions)
